text: FAIRFIELD-HEAD, a township in the p. of Allstonfield, Staffordshire, 71 m. NNE of Leek. Area 4,380 acres. Pop. 991.

FAIRFORD, a town and parish of England, in the co. of Gloucester, 24 m. SE of Gloucester, on the river Colne, near its confluence with the Thames. Area of p. 4,220 acres. Pop. 1,672. The church is an object of considerable interest; and is celebrated for its painted glass windows, said to have been captured in a ship bound from a Flemish port to Italy.

FAIRHAVEN, a bay on the NW coast of Spitzbergen, in N lat. 79° 50'.—Also a sea-port town in Bristol co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., situated in Buzzard's bay, 59 m. SE of Boston. Pop. 3,951. It has considerable shipping employed in the whale-fishery.—Also a township in Rutland co., in Vermont, watered by the Castleton. Pop. 633. -Also a v. in Newhaven co., in Connecticut, 38 m. S of Hartford, on the Quinnipiac. Pop. 1,000.— Also a v. in Preble co., in Ohio. Pop. 150.

FAIR-HEAD, a promontory in co. Antrim, near the NE angle of Ireland, and 21 m. S by E of the island of Rathlin or Raghery, in N lat. 55° 44', and W long. 6° 2'. This bold headland is composed of the most considerable mass of greenstone in the British islands. The greatest thickness of the bed is 260 ft.; its elevation above the sea 545 ft. The greenstone rests on strata of sandstone which dip SW. F., in consequence of its superior elevation, is distinguished, in the language of the country, by the name of Benmore; it forms the E termination of Ballycastle-bay, and is distant about 12 m. from the basaltic district of the Giant's causeway, known to mariners under the general name of Bengore-head. Nothing can form a more striking contrast than these two headlands. Bengore is composed of a series of distinct beds, some of which are formed of columns which for symmetry vie with architectural precision; while the rest present the most rugged and irregular aspect. At F., the bed of greenstone is continuous from top to bottom, and at the same time presents the appearance of columnar disposition; but this, on close examination, is found to be only an appearance, arising from the natural disposition of the greenstone to separate into distinct prismatic concretions, totally destitute of that uniformity of shape preserved in each individual column at the Giant's causeway. Such, however, is the tendency of the greenstone of F. to assume this structure, that some masses of not more than 20 or 30 ft. in diam., and nearly 200 ft. in length, may be observed almost entirely detached from the rest of the rock. Some of the columns are magnetical. The action of the great current, occasioned by the flowing of the tide through the Irish channel, which disgorges itself just at this point, where it is again met by the wave of the Atlantic, occasions a never-ceasing eddy along the shore of F., which prevents any accumulation of matter; and were its base not protected by the enormous fragments of greenstone which lie scattered over it, the soft material on which the promontory is supported would soon be washed away, and the whole launched into the ocean. Even as it is, the barrier formed by these enormous blocks is not entirely sufficient to resist the inroad of the waves; and from time to time great masses of the rock fall with a tremendous crash upon the debris below. The greenstone of F., like other beds of the same substance, is very fine grained, resembling basalt on the upper and the under surfaces, but coarse grained in the middle of the bed. This peculiarity is distinctly displayed on the under surface of the mass, where it comes in contact with the sandstone; but as no overlaying strata occur immediately along the summit, it is only at some distance from it that the fine grain is again observable. F. dips a little W by S, and is overlaid by sandstone at the distance of about a mile from the sea.

FAIR ISLE, an island lying betwixt Orkney and Shetland, 29 m. S by W of Sumburgh-head. It is upwards of 3 m. in length, and nearly 2 m. in breadth; and rises into three lofty promontories. It is everywhere inaccessible, save at one point upon the SE, where it affords a safe station for small vessels. One of the promontories, the Sheep-craig, is nearly insulated, rising from the ocean in a conical shape to the height of 480 ft. The soil is tolerably fertile, and the sheep-pasture on the hills excellent. In 1588 the flag-ship of the Duke de Medina Sidonia, the admiral of the Spanish armada, was wrecked on this island. Fair Isle is reckoned one of the Shetland islands, and is annexed to the ministry of Dunrossness. Pop. in 1801, 160; in 1841, 232.
FAIRLEE, a township and village of Orange co., in the state of Vermont, U.S., on the W bank of Connecticut river, 38 m. SE of Montpelier. Pop. 644.
FAIRLEY, a sea-port village of Ayrshire, 18 m. SW of Greenock. Pop. 521.
FAIRLIGHT, a parish in Sussex, 23 m. ENE of Hastings. Area 2,230 acres. Pop. 631.
FAIRMONT, a township in Luzerne co., in Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 594.
FAIRNESS SOUND, a harbour on the W coast of Eday, one of the Orkney islands.

FAIRPORT, a village in Elmira township, in Chemung co., state of New York, U. S., 192 m. SW of Albany. Pop. 500.—Also a v. in Painsville township, in Lake co., in the state of Ohio, on the E bank of Grand river, 178 m. NE of Columbus. It is the lake port of Painesville, with which it is connected by a railway 3 m. in length.-Also a v. in Ashtabula co., in Ohio. Pop. 641.
FAIRSTEAD, a parish in Essex, 4 m. WNW of Witham. Area 1,590 acres. Pop. 306.
FAIRTON, a village in Fairfield township, in the state of Connecticut, U. S., at the junction of Millcreek and Rattlesnake-river. Pop. 240.

FAIRVIEW, a township in Erie co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., bounded on the N by Lake Erie. Pop. 1,481.—Also a township in York co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 17 m. NW of York. Pop. 1,993.—Also a village in Brook co., in Virginia, 2 m. E of Ohio river. Pop. 200.
FAIRVILLE, a village in Arcadia township, in Wayne co., New York, U. S. Pop. 150.

FAIRWEATHER (CAPE), a cape on the E coast of Patagonia, in S lat. 51° 32', W long. 68° 55'. It very much resembles Cape St. Vincent on the coast of Spain, and also bears a great resemblance to Cape Virgins, 45 m. to the S.—Also a promontory on the W coast of N. America, terminated by a low blue cliff on a sandy beach, near which are some detached rocks, in N lat. 58° 51', W long. 137° 36'.

FAIRWEATHER (MOUNT), or CERRO-DE-BUENTEMPO, a high mountain on the W coast of N. America, about 12 m. NE of Cape Fairweather, on the frontier line between the Russian and British possessions. It is one of the principal summits of the Cordillera of New Norfolk. Its base is formed by the summits of the surrounding mountains, from which it rises to the height of 14,732 ft. above the level of the sea. It is covered with perpetual snow.
FAISANS (ILE DES), an island in the Bidassoa river, near Irun, 14 m. ESE of Saint Sebastian. The treaty of the Pyrenees was concluded here in 1659.
FAITH, an island in the Pacific, reported by FAL have been discovered by him on 31st Oct. 1843, in S lat. 21° 10′, W long. 138° 54′. It is 6 m. in circumf., and covered with cocoa-nut trees.
FAITH (SAINT), a parish in Hants, 1 m. S of Winchester. Pop. 438.
FAITHLEG, a parish in co. Waterford, 1 m. NNW of East Passage. Area 1,494 acres. Pop. 786.
FAITIE (CAPE), a cape on the W coast of the island of Borneo, in E long. 109° 30′, S lat. 0° 37′.
FAITS-LES-SENEFFE, a commune and v. of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 11 m. W of Charleroi. Pop. 975.
FAJAO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 9 m. SE of Arganil, on the l. bank of the Ceira.
FAJEMMIA, a town of Senegambia, 60 m. SE of Bambuk, in N lat. 12° 50'.
FAKENHAM (GREAT), a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. SSE of Thetford. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 213.

FAKENHAM-LANCASTER, a town and parish of Norfolk, situated on the river Wensum, 7 m. from E. Dereham. Area of p. 2,640 acres. Pop. 2,164. The town, which has a fine church, suffered considerably from fire in 1789.
FAKIRA, a town of Japan, on the island of Ximo, 15 m. S of Nangasaki.
FAKUL, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Caramania, 4 m. N of Konieh.
FAKUSIN, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 24 m. WNW of Ixo.

FAL, a river of Cornwall, rising in Tregossmoor, near the centre of the co., and flowing S and SW into the estuary which forms Falmouth harbour, after a course of 25 m., of which upwards of one-half is tidal. See FALMOUTH.

FALA AND SOUTRA, a united parish, of which Fala lies in the co. of Edinburgh, and Soutra in the co. of Haddington. Pop. in 1801, 354; in 1841, 393. The v. of Fala is 15 m. SE of Edinburgh. Soutrahill, which rises about 1,184 ft. above the level of the sea, part of the W ridge of the Lammermoor mountains, is covered for the most part with heath. To a traveller from the south, who has, over a considerable distance, traversed a dreary elevated moorland carpeted with heath, the road as it skirts this hill suddenly discloses the finely cultivated and beautiful expanse of the Lothians, with the estuary of the Forth, and the coast of Fife, in the background. On the summit of Soutra hill formerly stood the church and v. of Soutra, appropriately and graphically designated by that name, which signifies, in the Cambro-British, 'the hamlet with a prospect.'

FALABA, a town of Senegambia, 215 m. NE of Sierra-Leone, the cap. of the Sulima country. It derives its name from the river Fala, on which it stands. It is nearly 13 m. in length, by 1 m. in breadth; is closely built, and surrounded with a strong stockade, and a ditch 20 ft. deep; and contains about 4,000 circular clay huts, with pyramidal roofs of thatch. It was founded in 1768.—Laing.

FALAISE, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados.—The arrond., comprising an area of 87,047 hect., with a pop. in 1841, of 61,163; in 1846, of 61,658; is divided into the 4 cants. of Bretteville-sur-Laize, Coulibœuf, F., and Harcourt-Thury.—The cant. comprises 37 com. Pop. in 1831, 23,809; in 1841, 22,569.—The town is situated on the r. bank, and near the source of the river Anté, which flows into the Dives, in N lat. 48° 53' 55", W long. 0° 12' 9", 127 m. from Paris, through Versailles, Dreux, Verneuil, and Argentan. The castle of F. was one of the strongholds of the dukes of Normandy, and here William the Conqueror was born. It sustained 14 sieges at different times, from the early troubles of the duchy of Normandy, Capt. Simmons of the American brigantine Faith, to I until the war of the League, in which F. was taken by Henri IV. in person. Its fortifications, which were much injured in these attacks, are at present in a very dilapidated state; but the donjon of the castle, situated on a bold and lofty rock, is one of the proudest relics of Norman antiquity. The town stretches along the top of a rocky ridge rising abruptly from a fertile and well-wooded valley. The streets are wide, and public fountains impart a freshness to the appearance of the place. The pop. in 1831 was 9,581; in 1841, 8,109. It has considerable manufactures of lace, cotton-yarn, and hosiery. A large fair is held in the suburb of Guibray, which continues from the 10th to the 25th of August. F. has a tribunal of commerce, a communal college, an agricultural society, and a theatre.

FALASHA, or Falasja, a people of Abyssinia, who exhibit a variety of customs common to themselves and the Jews, and adhered to their religion when the rest of the kingdom embraced Christianity. They attempted to place a king of their own upon the throne, but were defeated, and driven to the mountains of Samen. At present individuals of this nation are found in all parts of Abyssinia; but the greatest number is on the banks of the Bahr-el-Abiad, among the Shilluks.
FALCES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 28 m. SW of Pampeluna, near the r. bank of the Arga. Pop. 2,310.
FALCET, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. WNW of Tarragona. Pop. 2,995.
FALCK, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Bouzonville. Pop. 498.
FALCON (CAPE), a promontory on the S coast of the island of Iviça, in N lat. 38° 50'.—Also a promontory of Algeria, in the prov. of Oran, in N lat. 35° 50′.
FALCON ISLANDS, two or three small islands near the coast of Connecticut, U. S., in Long island sound.
FALCONARA, a river of Sicily, in the Val-di- Noto.—Also a small town of Naples, in Calabria- Citra, 10 m. W of Cosenza, with 1,600 inhabitants, originally emigrants from Albania.
FALCONE, a cape on the NW coast of Sardinia, in N lat. 40° 59', E long. 8° 10'.
FALCONIERI, a small island in the Mediterranean, 26 m. NW of Milo, in N lat. 36° 50'.
FALCZI. See FALTSI.
FALDINGWORTH, a parish of Lincolnshire, 4 m. SW of Market-Raisen. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. in 1831, 296; in 1841, 350.

FALEME', a considerable river of W. Africa, being the largest of the tributaries to the Senegal. It rises near Curbari, in a basin surrounded with mountains, and about a league from its source is 80 paces wide. It flows along the W frontier of Bambuk, and falls into the Senegal 20 m. W of Galam, in N lat. 14° 40', after a course of 200 m. Its principal affluent on the r. is the Sanon-Kolez.—Mollien.
FALETSI, a village of Bessarabia, on an affluent of the Pruth, 50 m. NW of Kichenau.
FALFIELD, a chapelry of Gloucestershire, p. and 3 m. NE of Thornbury. Pop. 1,597.
FALIN, a river of Chinese Tartary, which runs into the sea of Japan, in N lat. 43° 5'.
FALISOLLE, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. and 9 m. SW of Namur. Pop. 746.
FALKEN, a village of Prussian Saxony, on the Werra, SE of Treffurt.
FALKENAU, a small but well-built town of Bohemia, on the Eger, in the circle of Elnbogen, 12 m. NE of Eger, and 66 m. W of Prague. Pop. 1,900.

FALKENBERG, an old seaport of Sweden, at the mouth of the Æthra or Falkenberg's-Aa, in the Cattegat, 52 m. N of Helsingborg. It consists of little more than 100 huts, covered with thatch. Pop. 1,660.—Also a small town of Prussian Silesia, FAL in the regency of Oppeln, on the Steinau, 14 m. W of Oppeln. Pop. 1,632.
FALKENBURG, a small town of Prussian Pomerania, in the circle of Dramburg, on the Drage, 32 m. E of Stargard. Pop. 3,050.
FALKENHAIN (UPPER), a village of Saxony, in the circle of Leipsig, NE of Wurzen. Pop. 666 .- Lower F. in the neighbourhood has a pop. of 500.
FALKENHAM, a parish of Suffolk, 9 m. ESE of Ipswich. Area 1,550 acres. Pop. 290.
FALKENSTEIN, a town and county of Lower Austria, in the circle of Korneuburg. The co. lies between the upper bail. of Alzey, the principality of Lautern, and the duchy of Deux-Ponts.
FALKENSTEIN, or Elleford, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau, 2 m. S of Auerbach. Pop. 3,346.
FALKENSWAART, a village of the Netherlands, in North Brabant, with 1,100 inhabitants, 7 m. S of Eindhoven.
FALKINGHAM, or FOLKINGHAM, a parish and village in Lincolnshire, 26 m. SSE of Lincoln. Area of p. 1,700 acres. Pop. 820.

FALKIOPING, or FALKÖPING, a small town of Sweden, in W. Gothland, 56 m. E of Uddevalla. Pop. 500. It is remarkable for a battle fought near it in 1388, between Albert king of Sweden, and Margaret queen of Denmark and Norway, in which the former was defeated, and made prisoner with his son.

FALKIRK, a parish and town in the E part of Stirlingshire. The p. at its NE end approaches within 3 m. of the Forth. The Forth and Clyde canal commences at this limit of the p., at Grangemouth, and runs SW past Grahamston and Camelon. About 41 m. from its commencement, at a point to the SW of Falkirk, where it has been raised by 16 locks from the level of the sea, it sends-off, on its S side, the Edinburgh Union canal.—The Edinburgh and Glasgow railway also intersects the p., and has a principal station about a 1 m. to the S of the town. The towns or villages, besides the burgh of Falkirk, and its suburb of Grahamston, are Grangemouth, Camelon, Laurieston, and Brainsford. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 8,838; in 1831, 12,743; in 1841, 15,621. The p. is notable in history as the scene of two important battles. The first battle of Falkirk was fought on the 22d of July 1298, between Scottish and English armies, headed respectively by Sir William Wallace and Edward I. of England. The second battle was fought on the 17th of January 1746, between 6,000 of the royal troops, and about an equal or probably superior number of the troops of Prince Charles Edward. The parliamentary burgh of F., the cap. of the E part of Stirlingshire, and a town of considerable importance, is situated in 55° 59' N lat., and 3° 44' W long.; 11 m. E of Stirling; 24 m. E by N of Glasgow; and 24 m. W of Edinburgh. It stands, as to its main body, on a gently rising ground, and sends off in different directions two elongated suburbs. On the N side is the v. or suburb of Brainsford, and the v. or suburb of Grahamston. These suburbs owe their rise to their being on the road to the great ironworks of Carron, leading down on the one side from these works, and on the other from F. to the most convenient point on the canal. F. is not in the strict sense a manufacturing town. Its principal manufacture appears to be leather; but even this is not of considerable extent. The town has no factories; but in 1838 it had 180 hand-looms for the weaving of cottons. The foundry at Grahamston, the basin and railway terminus at Brainsford, a distillery 1/2 a m. farther down the canal, intermediate extensive corn-mills, and a yard for the repair of barges and boats, employ many persons, and occasion stir and an appearance of prosperity in the suburbs. But the grand importance of F. consists subordinately in its being the depot of the internal trade for a considerable circumjacent district, and primely in its having great fairs or 'trysts'—the greatest in Scotland, and probably in Great Britain-for the exposure and sale of black cattle, sheep, and horses. The trysts are held thrice a-year, on the 2d Tuesday of August, the 2d Monday of September, the 2d Monday of October, and following days, generally for 3 days at a time. Pennant mentions that the number of cattle yearly exposed for sale at these trysts, when he visited Scotland in 1772, amounted to 24,000. At the October tryst in 1840, not fewer than from 80,000 to 90,000 black cattle, and from 90,000 to 100,000 sheep appeared upon the ground. The trysts are held about 3 m. to the NW of the town, on a large uncultivated field called Stenhouse-muir, in the p. of Larbert.-F. unites with Linlithgow, Hamilton, Lanark, and Airdrie, in returning a member to parliament. The number of registered electors in 1848 was 514.

FALKLAND, a parish in the Cupar district of Fifeshire, rising on the S into the East-Lomond hill [alt. 1,466 ft.], and the high ridge which connects it with the West-Lomond. Pop. in 1801, 2,211; in 1841, 2,886. A great part of the pop. in the town of F., and in the villages, are employed in the weaving of linen goods of different descriptions. Dowlas and sheeting are chiefly made for the manufacturers of Dundee, Newburgh, Cupar, and Ceres; diaper and towelling for the manufacturers of Dunfermline; and drills for those of Kirkcaldy and Dysart. — The town of F. stands at the NE base of the East-Lomond hill, and consists of one principal street, and some smaller streets and lanes. Its appearance, taken in connection with the palace and the church, is, notwithstanding some modern buildings, antique; and although now little better than a country-village, it must formerly have been a place of considerable importance, from the frequent residence of the royal family at the palace, during the reigns of the three last Jameses. The oldest portion of the palace of F., which was erected either by James or James IV., forms the S front, and is still partially inhabited. As completed by James V., the palace occupied three sides of a square court, the fourth or W side being enclosed by a lofty wall. The range of building on the N side of the court has entirely disappeared, and of that on the W, the bare walls only remain; these two portions of the palace having been accidentally destroyed by fire in the reign of Charles II. Richard Cameron, who for a time acted a conspicuous part in the resistance to Episcopacy in the reign of Charles II., was born in F.

FALKLAND ISLANDS, a group to the NE of the straits of Magalhaen, situated between the 51st and 53d parallel of S lat., and between 57° and 62° W long., at a distance of nearly 300 m. from the S extremity of the continent, so denominated by the English, in honour, it is supposed, of Lord Viscount Falkland; but by the French called the MALOUINES, and by the Spaniards, MALUINAS, from the people of St. Maloes, whom they esteem as the first discoverers. They were first seen by Davis in 1592; next in 1594, by Sir Richard Hawkins, who named the principal island Hawkins's Maidenland, in honour of Queen Elizabeth. In 1598, Sebald-de-Wert, a Dutch navigator, visited them, and gave them his own name, which they still bear in Dutch charts. Their present name has been adopted from accident or misapprehension from that of the sound or passage, through which Captain Strong first sailed in 1690, and which he called F. sound. In 1763, the French having lost Canada, turned their at- FAL tention to these islands as an American settlement in another quarter; and an ample description is given of them in Pernetty's account of Bougainville's voyage. In 1761, Commodore Byron was sent to take possession of them, upon the ground of prior discovery; and a small establishment was made at a place on Saunders's island called Port Egmont. The taking possession of these comparatively unimportant islands roused the ever-wakeful jealousy of Spain. A fleet was fitted out which dispossessed the British settlers in 1770; occasioned a counter-armament on the part of Great Britain; and had well-nigh led to a war between the two powers; but this dispute about islands, then considered as uninhabitable even by savages, was wisely settled by a convention in which Britain regained possession of them.—The islands are upwards of 200 in number, but only two are large; the others vary from 16 m. in length by 8 in breadth, to mere islets. They are deeply perforated by arms of the sea, and probably afford a greater number of fine natural harbours than any country of equal extent in the world. The shores afford fish of many kinds, amongst which is a species of grey mullet, weighing up to 15 lbs.; and seals are exceedingly numerous. The only noxious reptile is a species of centipede, which, however, is seldom seen.- No mineral productions except coal have yet been discovered; but good building-stone and roofing slate can be obtained with ease.—The only indigenous quadruped is the warrah or wolf-fox, which is about the size of an English hound, but by no means fierce. Wild fowl are very abundant, especially geese, swans, ducks, and teal.—Their climate very closely resembles that of Shetland. They have cold summers and mild winters. The temp. in winter is seldom more than 2° or 3° below the freezingpoint; but the chief defect of climate seems to be the absence of a high temp. in summer. It seldom exceeds 65°. The prevailing winds are from the W. Snow rarely exceeds 1 or 2 inches, and seldom lies in them; but owing to their humidity and want of sunshine, and the stormy winds which blow almost constantly, wheat or barley cannot be raised upon them, and even oats might turn out to be a precarious crop. It is doubted also if wood will grow; the bushes do not exceed 4 ft. in height; and the trees planted in 1845 around the Governmenthouse at Stanley harbour are now bare stumps. A great variety of sweet-scented flowers enamels the ground in November and December. Turnips and potatoes succeed well, and there is abundance of good grass, upon which many thousands of black long-horned cattle, horses, and hogs, thrive in a wild state; sheep have been introduced, and found to do well, though much of the land is too moist and swampy for them. The cattle average between 4 and 5 cwt. Extensive peat-bogs exist, which afford a sufficient supply of fuel. The pop. in 1840 consisted of 25 persons, young and old, one-half of them British, the other South American Indians of a semicivilized description. They were all stationed at Port-Louis, a fine natural harbour at the head of Berkeley sound, on the E coast of East F. In 1847 it amounted to 270. The greatest length of East Falkland island is about 95 m.; the mean, 85 m. The greatest breadth is about 53 m.; the mean, 40 m. The West Falkland is about 80 m. in length. Its breadth varies considerably, but 40 m. may be considered the greatest, and 25 the mean. The area of East F. may be roughly computed at about 3,000 sq. m.; that of West F. may be estimated at about 2,000 sq. m.; and that of the whole group, East and West F., with all the surrounding islands, at about 6,000 sq. m. A chain of high hills, which may almost be termed ザール・ファイス mountains, runs across East F. in a due E and W; direction from Port-William to Port-Sussex. Mount Usborne, the highest in the range, is about 2,300 ft. above the level of the sea; the other hills vary in height from 800 to 2,000 ft. The hills are nearly continuous, with but few passes between them; their summits are either in ridges or points, but seldom rounded, and never tabular. They are all formed of stratified quartz rock, and are extremely rugged at the summits, some of the rocks being of the most wild and fantastic forms. To the N of the chain just described, and in a parallel direction, is a lower range of hills of a similar character. Of this range Mount Simon, the highest, is computed at 1,600 ft. above the level of the sea. The N coast of East F., from Cape-Dolphin to Cape-Cary's fort, and extending back to the hills, is but slightly elevated, with a rocky shore and continuous line of cliffs of from 40 to 50 ft. in height. The surface is undulating. To the S, from the base of the hills to Eagle island inclusive, it is one continued undulating plain, deeply indented by sounds, harbours, and creeks; there is no decidedly flat land, but a series of shallow valleys, each with its rivulet. The shores are either muddy or sandy. All the islands to the S, and in the Straits, partake of the same character. Port-William readily communicates with either the N or S part of the island, being at the E extremity of the dividing range.—The San Carlos river in this island, is about 30 m. in length, and is navigable for 8 m.—The Matson has a course of 18 m. Of the interior of West F. there is not much known. From the coast the island appears upon the whole to be more hilly, and the hills to be more rounded, lumpy, and isolated than those in the East F. They appear to be varieties of quartz rock; and their height may be assumed as varying from 800 to 1,800 ft. The principal range is on the E side of the island, and runs in a contrary direction to those in East F.; its general bearing being N and S, extending from White Rock harbour to Fox-bay, parallel to the coast-line, and at a distance from it of about 3 m. The N coast of West F. consists of long islands, extending E and W, with narrow openings between them, of which Tamar-pass and the entrance to Port- Egmont are the most remarkable. Upon these islands are isolated hills, of conical and lumpy forms, rising abruptly from gently undulating ground. The islands further to the W, called the Jasons, are hills of this abrupt character, and from 800 to 1,200 ft. in height. [Governor Moody's Report.] Government. The seat of government has been removed from Port-Louis to Port-William, at the S entrance of Berkeley sound. The civil establishment of the islands consists of the governor, chief clerk, who also holds the office of clerk to the councils, second clerk, surveyor, colonial surgeon, who also is health officer for the port, harbour-master, and surveyor.—The judicial establishment consists of the stipendiary magistrate, who receives a salary of £400 per annum. He is chairman of the supreme court, which is held before him and one or more justices of the peace. The supreme court is, at present, the only court of judicature in the islands; in it are tried all matters, with the exception of such minor cases as would be decided by two justices-ofthe-peace in England. These cases are decided by the stipendiary magistrate alone. The supreme court sits four times a-year in the town of Stanley.—The ecclesiastical establishment consists of the colonial chaplain, who is the only clergyman in the colony; he receives a salary of £300 per annum out of the parliamentary grant.—There are two councils, the executive council, and the legislative council. The executive councillors are, at present, only 2 in num- | tween George island on the E, and Cape Meredith, FAL ber, and hold their places during the queen's pleasure. The legislative council is composed of the two justices whose names stand first in order in any general commission of the peace, such commission being renewable or revocable by the governor. The governor presides in each council.—The government of the colony is supported almost entirely by the parliamentary grant, the amount derived from other sources does not much exceed £1,000 per annum, and has chiefly arisen from the sale of beef; no taxes or duties are at present levied in the colony .-- A day-school has been established, at which 13 children, being the greater part of those in the town of Stanley, regularly attend. The schoolmaster receives an allowance from government of £20 per annum. The parents of the children also contribute towards his support 6d. a-week for each child. Commerce.] The commerce of the colony is, as may be supposed, exceedingly trifling; it consists chiefly of importing timber, lime, bricks, flour, sugar, coffee, and British manufactured goods; and exporting hides, seal-skins, and a few barrels of oil. The following average summary of exports, for the last 3 or 4 years, may be considered an approximation to the truth:- 400 hides, at 10s. each, 250 barrels of oil, at 45s. each, . The great object in establishing a colony here— which the British government is now doing at an expense of about £4,500 per ann.—is to provide a harbour-of-refuge and place of refitment for our ships frequenting the South seas, which are yearly increasing in number, and which can here be expeditiously and cheaply supplied with beef, water, fresh fish, wild fowl, and antiscorbutic vegetables. They are within 18 days' sail of St. Catherine's on the coast of Brazil, and 20 days' from Chiloé. Every vessel going to the Pacific ocean, or coming from it, passes within sight of these islands, or near them. At present 60 or 70 British ships pass these islands annually coming from Chili or Peru, and 20 more engaged in the whale-fishery. The trade to New South Wales employs about 300 vessels, most of which return to England by this route, which will also be preferred by vessels trading to New Zealand, Van Diemen's Land, Port-Philip, and probably South Australia. From Sydney or Hobart-Town the distance is nearly the same by Cape Horn or the Cape of Good Hope; but the prevalence of W winds in the parallels beyond the 30th, renders the latter route the more advantageous on the outward voyage, and the former on the return. The F. islands are about half as distant from Britain as New South Wales. There are several harbours about the main islands; and Port-Egmont is of such capacity as to afford anchorage for the largest fleet that the world can produce. Port-Louis, situated at the head of Berkeley-sound, is well-sheltered in all directions; the sound is about 15 m. deep and 4 broad, the soundings gradually shoaling from 20 to 4½ faths.; there are no dangers, except one sunken rock, about three quarters of a mile from Cape St. Vincent, and an admirable nautical chart of the group has been completed.

FALKLAND'S SOUND, a strait or bay, separating the two largest of the Falkland islands. Its N entrance lies between Race-point on the East F. and White-Rock point on the West F., in S lat. 51° 25', and has a width of 4 m. It opens on the S bethe extreme S point of West F., on the W, with a width of 30 m. Its extreme length from NNE to SSW, is about 65 m. It contains several islands, of which the largest are the Swan islands in S lat. 51° 46'.

FALL, a river of Oregon, rising near the parallel of 43° N, to the N. of Tlamath or Klamet lake, and flowing in a N by E course, along the E flank of the Cascade mountain-range, to the Columbia river, which it joins upon the l. bank, under the parallel of 45° 45′, 20 m. above the Dalles, after a course of about 170 m. It receives numerous tributaries on the l. bank, from the E flank of Mount Hood, Mount Jefferson, and several of the other principal summits of the Cascade range. These streams are characterized by the narrow and chasm-like valleys in which they run. The F. has its name from its falls, which occur near its junction with the Columbia.

FALL RIVER, a thriving township and village in Massachusetts, U. S., 51 m. S of Boston, situated on both sides of the Fall, at its entrance into Mount- Hope bay, a branch of Narraganset bay. Pop. in 1840, 6,738. It has print-works, fulling-mills, cotton-factories, and iron-works; and employs considerable shipping in the whale-fishery.—F. river consists of the outlet of Watuppa pond, 2 m. E of the town. It affords great water-power.
FALLAFAJIA, one of the Friendly islands, in the S. Pacific ocean, in S lat. 20° 30'.
FALLAIS, a commune and v. of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Hannut. Pop. 673.
FALLASCOSO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo- Citra, 18 m. SSW of Lanciano.
FALLEN CITY, or Broken Jerusalem, a range of rocks among the Virgin isles, SW of Virgin Gorda, in N lat. 18° 10'.
FALLERSLEBEN, a small town of Hanover, in the duchy of Luneburg, 27 m. SE of Zelle. Pop. 1,383.
FALLFORS, a town of Sweden, in W. Bothnia, on the Umeä river, 30 m. NW of Umeä.
FALLINGSBOSTEL, a village of Hanover, in the duchy of Luneburg, 14 m. NE of Rethem.
FALLING WATERS, a village in Berkeley co., in Virginia, U. S., 178 m. N of Richmond.
FALLOCH, a rivulet of Perthshire and Dumbartonshire, which rises on the NE side of Benchroan, in the co. of Perth, and runs into the N end of Loch-Lomond, after a rapid course of 9 m.
FALLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Villersexel. Pop. 611.
FALLOWFIELD, a town of Crawford co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 30 m. W of Philadelphia. Pop. 1,673.
FALMAGNE, a commune and v. of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, cant. of Dinant. Pop. 302.
FALMIGNOUL, a commune and v. of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 4 m. S of Dinant, on the Meuse. Pop. 340.

FALLS, a township of Wyoming co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., on the E side of the Susquehanna, 153 m. SE of Harrisburg. Pop. 2,108. — Also a township in Bucks co., in Pennsylvania, 25 m. NE of Philadelphia. Pop. 2,058.—Also a township in Hooking co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,625.
FALLSBURGH, a township in Sullivan co., in the state of New York, U.S., 197 m. S by W of Albany, on the Neversink river. Pop. 1,782.—Also
FALLSTON, a township in Beaver co., in Penn-
FALMER, a parish of Sussex, 4 m. WSW of Lewes. Area 2,840 acres. Pop. 493.

FALMOUTH, a parish, large sea-port, and cele- FAL brated packet-station, situated in the hund. of Kerrier, co. of Cornwall, 12 m. SSW of Tregony, 98 m. SW of Exeter, and 270 m. WSW of London, at the mouth of the river Fal. Area of the parish 1,210 acres. Pop. in 1801, 4,849; in 1831, 7,284, exclusive of that portion in Budock parish; in 1841, 7,695. -The town lies on the W side of the celebrated harbour of F., at the bottom of an eminence which commands the harbour; and consists principally of one continuous line of street, extending above a mile along the beach or quay, with suburbs branching inland from the main streets. Little F. and Flushing, on the opposite side of the inner harbour, which has an area of 522 acres, constitute a kind of suburbs to the town, with which they are in constant intercourse by means of a ferry. At the SE end of the town are convenient quays forming a basin, close to which are the custom-house, excise-office, and packetoffice, and warehouses for goods in bond; near the other end is Greenbank quay projecting out to lowwater mark, between which nearly the whole seafront of the town consists of an irregular line of wharfs with warehouses, to which ships may come to discharge or take in cargoes. This place has derived much importance from its having been, for 150 years, the principal station for the steam and other foreign mail-packets, and the general rendezvous for fleets and convoys bound to the S and W. The harbour is said to present the finest anchorage in England for steam or other vessels. The harbour is 4 m. long, and above 1 m. wide; and, on the E side, opposite St. Just, 14 faths. deep; it has many commodious creeks, and is so well-screened by the surrounding high lands, that the whole of the royal navy might be sheltered in it. The entrance is defended by Pendennis castle, consisting of works 1 m. in circuit, on the extreme W point, about a mile from F.; and on the E by the castle of St. Mawes, about 12 m. distant from Pendennis. Many fine navigable creeks branch from the harbour: the principal of which are, -Penryn creek, extending from Falmouth to Penryn; Restrongett creek, on the shores of which are numerous wharfs which are connected with the Redruth mining-district by railway; Truro creek, which is a branch of the main creek of the Fal; the Fal creek extending nearly to Tregony: another branch of it extends to Tresillian bridge; St. Mawes' creek, near the entrance of which and within St. Mawes' castle, on its N shore, stands the ancient borough which gives name to the castle and creek. The Fal opens into the ocean between Pendennis castle on the W bank, and St. Mawes' and Anthony point on the E; its channel being nearly a mile wide. The total area of the harbour and creeks is at least 10 sq. m. There is a lighthouse at the entrance to the harbour, in N lat. 50° 8', W long. 5° 1', with a revolving light elevated 65 ft. above high water.- The registered shipping belonging to the port in 1847 amounted to 6,518 tons. Vessels outward or inward bound generally touch at Falmouth for orders, and are at times repaired here. Steamers also readily obtain fuel and other supplies here. The prevalent winds here are from S to W, as is shown by the following table, viz.: Days. Days. Days. Days. Total, The municipal boundaries of the borough of F., as laid down in the boundary reports of 1835, extend over all the parish of F., F. harbour, parts of those of Mylor, including Little F. and Flushing, and of Budock, including the peninsular site of Pendennis castle in that p. The income of the borough, in 1839, was £285; in 1846, £331. F. was enfranchised by the reform act, and now, in union with Penryn, returns 2 members, as a parliamentary borough. The whole of Falmouth parish is included within the boundaries, which also include that part of Budock parish between Falmouth and Penryn, together with an extension of the old borough boundaries of Penryn. The number of electors registered in 1837 was 903; in 1848, 950. An extensive coasting-trade is conducted between London, Plymouth, Bristol, &c., and F.; as well as some foreign trade. The principal imports are timber, hemp, and tallow, from the north; fruits, wines, and spirits, from the south; rum and sugar from the West Indies; provisions and grain from Ireland. Tin, copper, pilchards, &c., are the chief exports. The gross receipts of customs' duty, in 1838, was £21,054; in 1846, £12,118. Shipbuilding and rope-making have been carried on at this port, though to no great extent.—The origin of F., as a town, though recent, is somewhat involved in obscurity. Leland, who visited Cornwall in the reign of Henry VIII., and who scrupulously notices not only every "praty," but every "poor fischar towne," speaks of Falmouth as "a havyn very notable and famose, and in a manner the most principale of al Britayne;" but says nothing of any town here. The town is first recorded by its present name in a charter of Charles II., bearing date 1661.
FALMOUTH, a small low lying island in the Chinese sea, in N lat. 11°.

FALMOUTH, a seaport of Massachusetts, U. S., situated in Barnstable co., between Buzzard's bay and Vineyard sound, 75 m. S by E of Boston. Pop. 2,070.—Also a township in Cumberland co., in the state of Maine, 6 m. N of Portland. Pop. 2,589.— Also a v. in Donegal township, in Lancaster co., in Pennsylvania, 15 m. SE of Haensburg.—Also a v. in Stafford co., in Virginia, 64 m. N of Richmond, on the Rappahannock. Pop. 500.—Also a v. in Pendleton co., in Kentucky, 40 m. SSE of Cincinnati.

FALMOUTH, a port of the island of Antigua, situated on a large bay called Falmouth harbour, 7 m. SE of St. John's.—Also a port of the island of Jamaica, situated in N lat. 18° 31', at the root of a peninsula stretching into the bay which forms the harbour of F. The nearest hills are about 3 m. to the S, and the intervening country is a complete marsh.—Also a thriving settlement in Nova Scotia, 25 m. NW of Halifax, on the W side of the Avon, which flows into the basin of Minas.
FALSA BAY, an inlet on the coast of La Plata, 30 m. N of the Rio Colorado. The surrounding country presents a dreary waste, and the bay itself is surrounded by sand banks.

FALSE BAY, an inlet on the coast of S. Africa, formed by the peninsula of the Cape of Good Hope on the NW side, and the land above False cape on the SE. The distance between its extreme points is 16½ m. Simon's bay, within this inlet, affords a safe station during the NW monsoon.

FALSE (CAPE), a cape on the coast of Yucatan, in the bay of Honduras, 10 m. NW of Cape Graciasa-Dios, and 35 m. ESE from the mouth of the Caratasca lagune.—Also a cape on the S coast of the island of Hayti, in N lat. 17° 28' 20", W long: 71° 40'.—Also a cape on the coast of Trinidad, 5½ leagues W of Cape Corrientes.—Also a cape on the E coast of Hindostan, at the mouth of the Mahana, in N lat. 20° 20'.- Also a cape on the coast of Africa, 4 m. S FAL 1/4 E of Cape Sierra Leone.—Also a cape at the E entrance of False bay, in S. Africa, in S lat. 34° 23' 48", E long. 18° 50' 20".—Also a low sandy cape on the W coast of Africa, 25 m. ENE of Cape Bojador.
FALSEGRAVE, a village and township in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 1 m. SW by W of Scarborough. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 545.

FALSTER, an island of Denmark, in the Cattegat, lying between the parallels of 54° 33' 26" and 54° 58′ 40" N, and between the meridians of 0° 24' 30" and 0° 52' 16" W of Copenhagen. It is 30 m. in length, with a breadth varying from 2 to 13 m. Area 185 sq. m. Pop. in 1846, 22,000. Though lying high, the surface is flat, well-watered, and wooded; and it is productive in various kinds of grain, pulse, potatoes, and in flax, hemp, and fruit. Honey forms an important article of export. Nykiöbing on the Guldborg-sound, and Stubbekiöbing on the N part of the island, on the Grön-sound, are the chief towns. It is administratively comprised in the bail. of Laaland, from which it is separated by the Guldborg-sound.
FALSTERBO, a seaport of Sweden, in the laen of Malmö, 22 m. SSW of Lund, in N lat. 55° 23' 11", E long. 12° 51′. Pop. 213 in 1833.
FALSTONE, a parish of Northumberland, 7 m. NW of Bellingham. Area 57,700 acres. Pop. 560.
FALSTYN, a small town of Austrian Galicia, 6 m. W of Sambor.
FALTERONA (MONTE-DI), a summit in the Tuscan Avennines, to the E of Florence. Alt. 5,380 ft.

FALTSI, a town of Moldavia, on the r. bank of the Pruth, 72 m. SE of Jassy. There are extensive ruins in the vicinity of this place, marking the site of an ancient town whose name has not been preserved. Peter the Great was defeated near this place by the Turks in 1711.
FALUGA, a small island in the Mediterranean, near the W coast of Sardinia, 12 m. S of Cape Argentiera.
FALUN, a small river of Switzerland, which runs into the lake of Neufchatel near Yverdun.
FALUN. See FAHLUN.
FALVATERRA, a village of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 10 m. SE of Frosinone.
FAMAGH, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 2 m. SSE of Thomastown. Area 492 acres. Pop. 106.

FAMAGOSTA, or FAMAGUSTA, a seaport town of the island of Cyprus, on the E coast, built on a low site, between two promontories, in N lat. 35° 7′ 40″, E long. 34°. It is surrounded by walls, and on the land-side by a deep ditch, 20 paces in breadth, cut out of the solid rock. The walls are thick, and flanked by 12 enormous towers, whose sides are 4 paces thick, and their interior 4 ft. in diam. The total circuit of the walls is nearly 2 m. Within the fortress there is a castle, with bastions, whereon a number of cannon are mounted. The town has two gates, with two drawbridges, one to the land-side, the other to the sea, which latter leads to the harbour. F. was fortified by Guy-de-Lusignan in 1193; and was still farther strengthened when in the possession of the Venetians and Genoese. But ruin and desolation are now seen in every street; and notwithstanding the extent of the walls, the number of its pop. is said not to exceed 200. Many of the churches are destroyed; others are abandoned and falling to decay; and the cathedral of St. Nicholas is now converted into the principal mosque. The harbour, which is large, and protected by some high rocks at its entrance, could once admit vessels of considerable draught, but is now choked-up with sand and rubbish. The adjacent country is sandy, bleak, and rocky.—F. is supposed to be built on the site of the ancient Arsinoe, so called from the name of the sister of Ptolemy Philadelphus, by whom it was founded. It is supposed to have derived its present name from Cape Amochostos, 5 m. to the S of Salamis. [Kinneir.] Here the Lusignans caused themselves to be crowned kings of Jerusalem. After belonging for a considerable time to different states of Italy, it was besieged by the Turks in 1570, and after having sustained six assaults, it surrendered by capitulation in 1571. The conditions were disgracefully violated by the victor, who caused the companions of the commander to be inhumanly massacred, and the gallant Bregandino himself to be flayed alive. The Ottoman army engaged in the siege of F. consisted of bomb-shells were expended, which nearly two centuries afterwards were still seen lying in heaps about the town!

FAMARS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, 3 m. S of Valenciennes. Pop. 597. The French had a fortified camp for the defence of Valenciennes near this place, in 1793. It was attacked on the 23d of April, and the French driven from the ground, which enabled the Allies to lay siege to Valenciennes.

FAMATINA, a department and town in the Buenos-Ayrean prov. of La Rioja. Pop. of dep. about 6,000. A considerable quantity of wine is grown in this dep., which takes its name from the mineral range of F., which is distant from La Rioja about 35 leagues W, and is seen to extend for 50 leagues, having in its centre a Nevado, or lofty peak covered with perpetual snow. Its geological formation is chiefly gneiss and clay-slate; and it is specially celebrated for the richness of its silver ores, which, it is reported, extend over an area 14 leagues in length, by 10 or 12 in breadth. The chain in fact consists of numerous mountain-ridges, each containing veins of different metallic ores.
FAMBRIDGE (NORTH and SOUTH), two contiguous parishes in Essex. North F., 5 m. NNW of Rochford, has an area of 2,100 acres. Pop. 142.— South F. has an area of 1,670 acres. Pop. 94.
FAMELICAO (VILLA-NOVA-DE), a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Minho, comarca and 9 m. SE of Barcellos. Pop. 2,000.
FAMERCK, or Felmer, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Thionville. Pop. 1,125.

FAMIA, or FAMIEH, a town of Syria, anciently called Apamea, on the r. bank of the Orontes, 120 m. N of Damascus. The Seleucidæ had their cavalry establishment here; and 30,000 horses and 500 elephants were supported from the produce of the surrounding territory: now the marshy soil will hardly support a few buffaloes and sheep.
FAMILY ISLANDS, a cluster of small islands near the NE coast of New Holland, 12 m. NW of Cape Sandwich. The NE island, which rises to a peaked summit of moderate height, is in S lat. 18° 2′ 9″.

FAMINE (PORT), a harbour in the strait of Magalhaens, in S lat. 53° 38′ 12″, W long. 70° 54′ 01″. [King.] In 1584, the Spanish navigator, Pedro de Sarmiento, entered the straits, and founded a town, named Jesus, the site of which, Captain King thinks, was between the first and second narrows of the strait; but according to the old navigators, was in the vicinity of Cape Possession. He then proceeded 40 leagues further S, and founded the town of San- Felipe; but on his embarking to return to the settlement at Jesus, he was driven to sea, and compelled to shape his course to Rio-de-Janeiro. From that place he made several unsuccessful attempts to carry FAN succours to the unhappy settlers on the straits, who were rapidly cut off by privation, disease, and the arrows of the natives. In 1587, Cavendish, entering the strait, destroyed the town of San-Felipe, and gave the adjacent harbour the name, which it still bears, of Port F. "This," says Captain King, "was the first, and perhaps will be the last, attempt made to occupy a country offering no encouragement for a human being; a region where the soil is swampy, cold, and unfit for cultivation, and whose climate is thoroughly cheerless." The mean temp. and pressure at Port F. in 1828 was as follows: Temp. Pressure. February, August, An interesting account of Sarmiento's hapless colony, and of the surrounding region, is given by Captain King in the Voyages of the Adventure and Beagle, vol. i. chap. 3.
FAMMAMATZ, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 60 m. ESE of Okasaki. It contains about 1,200 houses.
FAMOE, an island of Denmark, off the N coast of Laaland, 54 m. NNW of Saxkiobing, in N lat. 54° 59'. It is about 3 m. in length, and 13 m. broad. Pop. 380.
FAMPOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Arras. Pop. 1,005.
FANADO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, an affluent of the Araçuahi.
FANAGORIA. See TAMAN.
FANANO, a town in the duchy of Modena, in the cant. and 1 m. SSE of Sestola. Pop. 2,100.
FANARAKI, a village of Turkey, in the sanj. of Viza, 2 hours NW of Buyukdere.
FANARI, a port of Albania, at the entrance of the gulf of Arta, 6 leagues SE of Parga.
FANDAH. See Fundah.
FANE, a river of cos. Armagh, Monaghan, and Louth, rising in Lough Mackno and other lakes, and running SE to Dundalk-bay, into which it flows, after a course of 20 m.
FANEFIELD, a mountain of Norway, in the island of Senjen, bail. of Finmark. Alt. 4,284 ft.
FANFUS, one of the Navigators' islands, in the S. Pacific ocean, in W long. 170° 20', S lat. 14° 4'.
FANG, a town and district of China, in the provof Hu-pi, 60 m. S of Yun-yang.
FANGFOSS, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. NW of Pocklington. Area 980 acres. Pop. 185.
FANG-KI, an island off the S coast of China, in N lat. 21° 18′.
FANG-SHANG, a town of China, in the prov. of Chih-li, 39 m. WSW of Pekin.
FANIMBU, a small village of Bambarra, in Africa, 130 m. NW of Sego.
FANJEAUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, 13 m. W of Carcassonne, at an alt. of 1,200 ft. above sea-level. Pop. of town 1,853; of cant. 9,740.
FAN-KI, a town of China, in the prov. of Shansi, on the Hu-to-ho river.
FAN-LING-TAO, an island of Japan, off the E coast of Corea, in N lat. 36° 45'.
FANLOBBUS, a parish in co. Cork, comprising the town of Dunmanway. Area 35,606 acres. Pop. in 1831, 11,405; in 1841, 12,253.
FANNET, a township in Franklin co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 16 m. N of Chambersburg, watered by the Tuscarora. Pop. 1,858.
FANNETSBURG, a village in Franklin co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 59 m. S by W of Harrisburg.
FANNICH (LOCH), a lake of Ross-shire, in the p. of Contin, 9 m. in length, and from 1 to 11 m. in breadth.

FANNIN, a county in the state of Texas, U.S., situated on the Red river, and separated by Boisd'Arc creek from Red river co. Its soil is welladapted to the cultivation of cotton and Indian corn; a considerable portion is prairie.
FANNISSIMA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 100 m. NW of Jedo, and 155 NE of Meaco.

FANO, a well-built town of the Papal states, in the deleg. of Urbino-e-Pesaro, 7 m. SE of Pesaro, on the Adriatic, at the embouchure of the Arzilla, in N lat. 43° 51′ 16". It is a bishop's see. Pop. 6,800. It is surrounded with a high brick wall, and contains a large square, a cathedral, and several churches with elegant paintings, an academy, a library, and a large opera-house. It has manufactures of plain silk ribbon. The town contains several remnants of antiquity, among others the ruins of a triumphal arch. It was anciently called Fanum Fortunce, from a temple built to Fortune by the Romans, after the memorable defeat of Asdrubal on the banks of the Metaurus near this town. In its vicinity, too, Narses obtained a victory over Theia, king of the Goths, who was killed in the battle. F. was destroyed by Totila, and rebuilt by Belisarius.-Also a small town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 2da, 15 m. NE of Civita-Ducale.

FANO, a small island, or rather rock, off the SW coast of the island of Corfu. It is about 12 m. in circuit; and measures 4½ m. in its greatest length. It consists of two parts, one a mountainous limestone tract, rising to the alt. of 1,500 ft. above sea-level, and including a mountain-valley of basin-like form; the other, an irregular low hilly surface, entirely destitute of plain, and composed chiefly of marl and sandstone. The honey of F. is excellent; the vine and Indian corn are cultivated, and a few sheep and goats are reared. Though small, this island is important in a political sense, as it overlooks the adjacent navigation of the Adriatic: not a sail can pass, however near to Corfu or Albania, without being noticed from it. A small detachment of British troops is stationed upon it.
FANO (CAPE), a promontory of Norway, in N lat. 70° 30′.

FANOE, an island of Denmark, near the SW coast of N. Jutland, opposite to the town of Rypen, in N lat. 55° 23'. It is low and sandy, and about 15 m. in circumf., and has considerable shipping. Pop. in 1840, 3,000, chiefly fishermen.
FANSHAW (CAPE), a cape on the W coast of N. America, on the N side of Frederick's sound, in N lat. 57° 11'.
FANTARAZE, a river of Madagascar, flowing into the bay of Teintingue, opposite the island of St. Mary, after a course from W to E of 15 m.

FANTI, a district of Upper Guinea, on the Gold coast, between the meridians of 0° 30′ and 1° 30′ W; bounded on the N by Assin; on the E by Aguna; and on the W by Wassaw. It is watered by the Shama and Amissa; and its coast is the seat of several of the principal trading-factories. The influence of the Fantis extends along the coast from Cape-Coast-castle to the frontier of Acra, a space of about 100 m., and nearly as far inland to the frontiers of Ashantee. The F. were originally subject to the Ashantees. Their form of government is republican; justice being administered by the pynins, who are generally chosen by the public voice. The dress of both sexes consists of a piece of cloth wrapped loosely about the body. The women usually appear with their breasts PRATE 1- FAR exposed; their dress is fastened round the waist by a girdle or zone, called a tombah, to which women of rank have a number of silver keys suspended. A Fanti may be known from other Africans by small scarifications on the upper part of the cheek-bones, and on the back of the neck. Since 1811 the independence of this people has been shaken by repeated and formidable invasions of the Ashantees.
FAN-YUAN, a town of Corea, 23 m. ESE of Koang-tcheo.
FANZARA, a village of Fez, 15 m. S of Sali.
FAO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Minho, comarca and 6 m. W of Barcellos, on the l. bank of the Cavado.

FAOU (LE), a canton and seaport town of France, in the dep. of Finistère, on the bay of Brest. The town, 14 m. E by S of Brest, had a pop. of 877 in 1841; the cant. of 6,637. The port had 47 vessels = 476 tons belonging to it in 1841.

FAOU-CHU-FU, a district of China, in the prov. of Kiang-si, the cap. of which is situated in N lat. 28° 59' 20", E long. of Pekin 0° 13' 38". It contains the city of King-te-ching, celebrated for its china-manu factures.
